raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a beautifully plated dish of thinly sliced cooked chicken breast, garnished with green onions, mustard, and wasabi. A male voice-over reflects, "like hearing his explanation, seeing his preparation and knowing that he thinks that he probably hasn't killed anybody, that was enough for me to feel pretty secure," as dramatic instrumental music begins. The scene transitions to a cozy Japanese restaurant where a chef in a blue apron stands behind a counter, while a man in a 7-Eleven headband engages in conversation. The narrator announces, "Our cross-country Japan food tour continues. As we move from Sendai to Fukushima," as a white and yellow train arrives at a bustling platform, followed by a map highlighting the route. A vibrant shot of the man joyfully leaping in a golden field with mountains then gives way to a somber view of a devastated coastal area, with destroyed buildings and debris. The narrator explains, "I came here to see if life has returned back to normal. After the tragic tsunami and nuclear disaster that occurred eight years ago." A female voice adds, "There was a huge earthquake, which caused tsunami to come, and then the tsunami affected the nuclear reactor." The narrator then asks, "How did Japan recover from that?" followed by an energetic shout of "Let's go!" Upbeat traditional Japanese music starts as the video returns to a serene rural setting with the man and a woman in a golden field. A car drives on a highway under a sign for Niigata, Aizuwakamatsu, and Kikukawa. The narrator states, "While we're at it, we're stepping back into Japanese traditions that have withstood the test of time," as the man and woman are seen pounding rice in a large wooden mortar to make mochi. The man, wearing the 7-Eleven headband, is heard saying, "If you trust me, I want to see how tight of a pattern we can get," followed by a countdown, "Three, two, one." The narrator continues, "Including unique foods, you wouldn't expect to see in Japan," as a sizzling pan of stir-fried vegetables is shown. The video concludes with the man and woman laughing together, with the woman playfully asking, "Can I eat it if I don't look at it?" and the man responding, "Yeah, I could drop it in your mouth."

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with an aerial view of a valley and a town, accompanied by upbeat music, as a male voice questions the presence of koi on a dinner plate and asks for its Japanese translation. The scene shifts to an industrial-looking space accessed via a metal hatch, where a chef in a white uniform uses a strainer to scoop from a deep pit, while a man in a blue shirt and headband gestures. Adventurous music plays with a train horn and rattling sounds as a braised dish is lifted with chopsticks. Inside a train, the man in the blue shirt sleeps while a woman looks out the window. An aerial shot of Kitakata, Fukushima, reveals golden rice fields, and a car drives through the countryside. A narrator describes the quiet ambiance and the smell of vegetation, hinting at a unique creature in the fields for an unheard-of dish, focusing on a butterfly and bee. Shizuka Anderson, in a gray jacket and green cap, stands in a rice field with a woman in a yellow top, asking their location (Kitakatashi, Fukushima) and if they will catch grasshoppers, noting his unprepared attire. He crouches to examine rice plants with a clear container. The narrator identifies the man with the headband as Masamichi Igawa, stating he 'knows everything.' The video then shows a gloved hand inspecting rice stalks and a caterpillar, with narration about countryside living and grasshoppers, alongside viewer comments about radiation in fish. Shizuka Anderson explains that his videos about Japan receive comments about radiation, prompting him to ask about Japan's recovery. In an industrial room, individuals in white suits sort debris, while a Japanese male voice confirms recovery ('復活してます'), and the woman in yellow elaborates on volunteer efforts and the years it took to rebuild. As a blue flower with dew is shown, the Japanese speaker states there is 'almost no danger' ('今は、もうほとんど危険はなくて'), with the woman translating that locals eat raw vegetables and that rice screenings are safe, accompanied by hopeful music. An aerial shot of rice paddies, houses, and mountains illustrates the landscape, as Shizuka acknowledges the ongoing work to regain trust. A bright yellow grasshopper in a net is shown, and Shizuka declares it 'good to go,' followed by a final scene of Shizuka, the woman in yellow, and a man in a blue shirt with a 7-Eleven headband smiling in the field. The video opens with a close-up of a yellow grasshopper on a green leaf, with the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband narrating about wanting to be caught. The scene shifts to a wide shot of a golden rice field where the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband and the Woman in Yellow Dress examine crops. The woman exclaims catching one, then it flies away, but the man confirms she got a huge one, asking about the adrenaline rush. A score overlay appears: 'SONNY 6 - 1 SHIZUKA', with upbeat music. A close-up of a white container with plant matter is shown, followed by an aerial view of rice fields and mountains. The scene transitions to a traditional Japanese house where Masamichi narrates about the 'secret ingredient' and 'Japan's riskiest food-making procedure.' Two men pound rice in a wooden mortar with mallets, joined by the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband and the Woman in Yellow Dress. The video opens with the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband, wearing a 7-Eleven headband, explaining the mochi-pounding process involving two people with hammers and one person sticking their hand in. The Woman in Yellow Dress playfully warns him to 'Be scared.' He introduces mochi, detailing its preparation from soaking, straining, and steaming rice before pounding. The Man in Blue Shirt and White Headband aims for a tight pattern as rhythmic pounding begins. The Woman in Yellow Dress declines participation, and a countdown initiates intense pounding. The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband takes turns, grunting with exertion, while close-ups show the sticky rice being mashed. The group laughs, and the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband describes the 'crazy sticky' product. The Woman in Yellow Dress expresses it's 'more than I can chew' as the rice is shaped into a mochi strip. They all taste the mochi, with sounds of eating and appreciation. The video opens with three people—the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband, the Woman in Yellow Dress, and a man in a navy blue t-shirt—tasting white mochi outside a traditional Japanese building, expressing delight. The Man in Blue Shirt describes the mochi as 'unrefined' and 'sticky,' while the Woman in Yellow Dress calls it 'pure mochi.' The scene transitions to hands stretching mochi, then to a Traditional Japanese Kitchen where a Woman in Green Cardigan prepares mochi soup with broth. Narrated details explain mochi's versatility, including mochi soup, red bean paste, soybean flour dessert, and natto topping. The group, seated around a Low Wooden Table on Tatami Mats in a Traditional Japanese Dining Room, enjoys the meal. The Man in Blue Shirt asks a new diner his name, who replies in a Japanese accent as Yujiro Iwata, stating he 'can't speak English.' The narrator introduces him as 'Mr. Yuchiro' and his wife, Asami. A close-up of a Grasshopper Jar filled with live yellow grasshoppers is shown, as the Man in Blue Shirt asks the Woman in Yellow Dress if she's ever had a bug, to which she responds 'Never.' The narrator concludes, 'This is what we came here for. The grasshopper is boiled in...', returning to the group's dining experience.

Chef in White Uniform.description: A chef wearing a white uniform and hairnet, handling a large pot in a kitchen, later using a strainer to scoop from a deep pit, indicating a traditional cooking method.

Kitakata, Fukushima.description: An expansive aerial shot of Kitakata, Fukushima, with golden rice fields stretching into the distance, a location explicitly named by a female voice as 'Kitakatashi, Fukushima' and referred to by a narrator as 'Fukushima prefecture' where 'everything seems normal' and a 'unique creature' resides in the fields.

Woman in Yellow Dress.description: A woman in a yellow long-sleeve top and jeans, initially tasting mochi with visible enjoyment, describing it as "pure mochi, freshly pounded rice cake, with no flavoring added," and later seated at the dining table, laughing, saying "No" when asked about eating something on her arm, and responding "Oh no" and "Never" when asked about eating a bug. Her voice is distinct and expressive.

Butterfly in Rice Field.description: A butterfly with black and white wings resting on golden rice stalks, highlighting the natural beauty and biodiversity.

Golden Field.description: A vast, sunlit field of golden crops with mountains in the background, where the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband and Woman in Yellow Dress interact, with close-ups of insects and flowers, emphasizing the natural and agricultural setting.

Man with Headband.description: A man wearing a white headband with a 7-Eleven logo and a dark blue shirt, seen in a kitchen handling a large pot and later asleep on a train. He is identified by the narrator as Masamichi Igawa, who 'knows everything' about the unique Japanese dish being prepared.

Clear Container.description: A transparent plastic bottle held by Sonny, used to playfully spray water onto the plants.

Shizuka Anderson.description: A man in a gray jacket and green cap, identified as Shizuka Anderson from 'Japan by Food,' seen in a golden rice field with a woman in a yellow top and Sonny, engaging in conversation and examining rice plants. He is part of a playful competition with Sonny, as indicated by the 'SONNY 10 - 0 SHIZUKA' score overlay, and is heard saying 'I'll try my best!' and 'I'm trying.'

Aerial Valley View.description: An expansive aerial shot of a lush green valley with a small town nestled among rolling hills, featuring a winding road and dense forest, under a soft, overcast sky.

Woman in White Tank Top.description: A woman in a white tank top, seated beside the Man with Headband, listening attentively, smiling, and responding with hand gestures, appearing engaged and amused by the interaction.

Bee in Rice Field.description: A bee with a black and yellow body perched on a rice stalk, emphasizing the ecosystem of the rice fields.

Highway Scene.description: A car driving along a highway, passing through the countryside, with mountains in the background, reinforcing the journey theme.

Braised Dish.description: A close-up of a glossy, braised dish being lifted with chopsticks, emphasizing the rich, savory food being prepared.

Metal Hatch.description: A close-up of a metal hatch with a diamond plate pattern being opened, revealing a dark, industrial-looking space beneath, suggesting a hidden or underground element.

Train Interior.description: The interior of a moving train with orange and beige patterned seats, where a man is asleep and a woman looks out the window, accompanied by the distinct sounds of a train horn and rattling.

Deep Pit.description: A dark, deep pit in a kitchen, from which a chef uses a strainer to scoop something, indicating a traditional or unique cooking method.

On-Screen Comments.description: Text overlays from viewers, including 'Nuclear hybrid fish lol hahahahaha be careful in Japan' and 'How much radiation in that food?', appearing over scenes of a fish and rice field, reflecting public concern about food safety in Japan, which Shizuka Anderson directly addresses in his narration.

Yellow Grasshopper in Net.description: A bright yellow grasshopper being held in a black mesh net, shown in close-up, observed while Sonny discusses 'catching grasshoppers' and confirms 'There's a grasshopper.'

Man in Blue Shirt with Headband.description: A man in a blue polka-dotted shirt and a white headband with a 7-Eleven logo, actively participating in tasting mochi, describing it as "so weird," "unrefined," "sticky and clumpy," and later seated at a low wooden table, engaging in conversation, asking "Are you gonna eat this part on your arm?" and "Have you ever had a bug?", and introducing Yujiro. His voice is distinct and conversational.

Industrial Cleanup Scene.description: A dimly lit, industrial-looking room with individuals in white protective suits sorting through debris and trash, visually representing the cleanup effort that the Woman in Yellow Dress describes as a 'huge effort' by 'tons of volunteers' to 'clean up, rebuild' over 'years'.

Blue Flower with Dew.description: A close-up of a small blue flower with dew drops on its petals and surrounding green stems, highlighting the natural beauty and moisture in the rice field.

Score Overlay.description: A graphic overlay displaying 'SONNY 6 - 1 SHIZUKA' with circular profile images of the two men, indicating a playful competition or game during the field inspection.

Yellow Grasshopper.description: A bright yellow grasshopper perched on a large green leaf in the rice field, shown in close-up, which is the subject of narration by the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband about catching it for collection.

Traditional Japanese House.description: A traditional Japanese house with wooden beams, sliding doors, and a small blue sign with Japanese text, serving as the backdrop for the outdoor mochi-making activity.

White Container with Plant Matter.description: A white container filled with green plant matter, possibly for agricultural inspection or collection, shown in close-up, suggesting the examination of crops.

Wooden Mortar and Mallets.description: A large, weathered wooden mortar and matching mallets used for pounding rice, shown in close-up and in action, central to the traditional mochi-making process, producing distinct rhythmic pounding sounds.

Man in Blue Shirt and White Headband.description: A man in a blue shirt and white headband, crouching beside the wooden mortar, guiding the pounding process, and later shaping the pounded rice into a mochi strip for tasting. He is heard saying 'Man, if you trust me, I want to see how tight of a pattern we can get' and 'Go go go go go' while guiding the pounding.

Traditional Japanese Dining Room.description: A dimly lit room with tatami flooring, wooden walls, and sliding shoji screens, decorated with framed calligraphy and a large, colorful mask, where the group enjoys a communal meal.

Tatami Mats.description: Traditional Japanese floor mats made of woven straw, upon which the diners sit on cushions around the low wooden table, adding to the authentic atmosphere.

Grasshopper Jar.description: A clear plastic jar filled with live yellow grasshoppers, submerged in liquid, placed on a table. A narrator states, "This is what we came here for," referring to the grasshopper, which is "boiled in..." The group also discusses eating "a bug."

Woman in Green Cardigan.description: A woman in a green cardigan over a black top, preparing mochi by stretching it over a bowl and serving broth, later seated at the table, smiling and participating in the meal.

Low Wooden Table.description: A low, polished wooden table set on tatami mats, surrounded by diners seated on cushions, with an array of small blue-and-white patterned bowls and plates filled with mochi, natto, and broth.

Traditional Japanese Kitchen.description: A cozy kitchen with wooden cabinets, a large window with frosted glass, and a wooden table set with various dishes, including a large metal bowl, plates of mochi, and a pot of broth.

Natto.description: A traditional Japanese dish of fermented soybeans, shown in a black bowl over rice, with a glossy, stringy texture, being eaten with chopsticks. A narrator describes it as "Japan's infamous sticky fermented soybean."

Mochi.description: A soft, white, chewy rice cake, shown being stretched, folded, and served in various forms—plain, topped with red paste, or with natto—emphasizing its elasticity and texture. It is described by the group as "unrefined," "sticky," "clumpy," "pure," and "freshly pounded rice cake, with no flavoring added." A narrator further details it as a "carby, amorphous base" that can be made into "mochi soup" with vegetable broth, topped with "red bean paste," coated in "soybean flour" for a "yellow vibrant dessert," or topped with "natto."

Fried Mochi.description: Golden-brown, crispy mochi pieces coated in breadcrumbs, topped with a dark, glossy sauce, served on a textured gray plate.

Blue-and-White Patterned Bowls.description: Small ceramic bowls with blue floral patterns, used for serving individual portions of broth, natto, and other side dishes at the dining table.

raw_segments: The video opens with an aerial view of a lush green valley, showcasing a small town nestled among dense forests, as upbeat, slightly mysterious music begins. A male voice introduces a fish dish "never expected to see on a dinner plate," then asks, "what is koi in Japanese?" The scene transitions to a close-up of a metal hatch being opened, revealing a dark, industrial-looking space beneath. Inside a kitchen, a chef in a white uniform and hairnet handles a large pot, while a man in a blue shirt with a headband gestures animatedly. The chef then uses a strainer to scoop something from a deep, dark pit, hinting at a traditional cooking method. The music becomes more adventurous as a male voice sings, "Come along as we carry the load to every road and country code, say!" accompanied by a train horn and rattling sounds. A close-up of a glossy, braised dish being lifted with chopsticks emphasizes the rich, savory food. The scene shifts to a train interior, where the man in the blue shirt and headband is asleep, while a woman in a white tank top sits beside him, looking out the window, with train sounds continuing. The music then shifts to a lighter, traditional Japanese sound as an expansive aerial shot of Kitakata, Fukushima, reveals golden rice fields stretching into the distance. A car drives along a highway, passing through the countryside with mountains in the background. A narrator states, "As we set foot in Fukushima prefecture, everything seems normal," describing a "quiet countryside ambiance" and "the smell of vegetation," before hinting that "deep inside these fields lies a unique creature" that will become part of an "almost unheard of Japanese dish." The camera focuses on a butterfly and a bee in the rice fields, highlighting natural beauty. The scene transitions to Shizuka Anderson from 'Japan by Food,' in a gray jacket and green cap, standing in the rice field with a woman in a yellow top. Shizuka asks, "Here we are. We are in a field in where, where are we again?" to which the woman responds, "Kitakatashi, Fukushima." Shizuka then asks if they're going to "catch grasshoppers today," humorously noting his unprepared attire compared to her gloves, prompting her laughter. He gestures widely and then crouches to examine the rice plants, holding a clear container. The narrator concludes by identifying the man with the headband as "Masamichi Igawa," stating he "knows everything."

The video opens with a close-up of a gloved hand holding a clear plastic container, inspecting golden rice stalks, and a green caterpillar crawling on leaves, as the narrator introduces 'countryside living' and the topic of 'grasshoppers.' This is followed by a close-up of a large, dark fish being held open, overlaid with on-screen comments like 'Nuclear hybrid fish lol hahahahaha be careful in Japan' and 'How much radiation in that food?', reflecting viewer concerns about food safety, even as the narrator mentions the historical consumption of grasshoppers. Shizuka Anderson, in a gray jacket and green cap, is then seen in a golden rice field with a woman in a yellow top, holding a clear container, as he explains that his videos about Japan and Japanese food often receive comments from people 'freaked out about radiation in food' due to past events, prompting him to ask how Japan recovered. The scene shifts to a dimly lit, industrial-looking room where individuals in white protective suits sort debris, while a Japanese male voice confirms recovery ('復活してます') and the woman in yellow elaborates that 'tons of volunteers' helped 'clean up, rebuild,' a 'huge effort' that 'took years.' As a blue flower with dew drops is shown, the Japanese speaker adds that 'now, there is almost no danger' ('今は、もうほとんど危険はなくて'), with the woman in yellow translating that locals now eat raw vegetables and that screenings and tests for rice have made it safe, accompanied by a gentle, hopeful background music. An aerial shot of expansive golden rice paddies with houses and mountains, and a bird flying, illustrates the rural landscape as Shizuka Anderson acknowledges that despite thorough checks, 'there's still a lot of work to be done in winning back the trust of other regions.' The segment concludes with a close-up of a bright yellow grasshopper in a net, which Shizuka Anderson declares 'good to go,' followed by a final scene of him, the woman in yellow, and a man in a blue shirt with a 7-Eleven headband smiling and interacting in the field, as an off-screen female voice compliments 'Chizzy' (Shizuka) for 'pushing yourself to do new things.'

The video opens with an expansive aerial view of golden rice fields in Kitakata, Fukushima, stretching towards distant mountains under an overcast sky, accompanied by upbeat, adventurous background music. The scene transitions to a dirt path where Shizuka Anderson, a man in a blue shirt with a 7-Eleven headband (Sonny), and a woman in a yellow long-sleeve top and light blue jeans walk away from the camera, engaging in conversation. The woman laughs, exclaiming, "I'll try my best!" as Sonny explains, "Catching grasshoppers is pretty easy. Right now we're literally walking past thousands of them." They stop at the field's edge, crouching to examine rice plants and other green crops. Sonny, holding a clear plastic bottle, instructs, "Spot your prey. Oh, sh*t, there's big ones here! Move swiftly." Close-ups show their hands interacting with rice stalks and a large green leafy plant with red flowers. Sonny counts, "Here we go. One, two," followed by the woman's "Oh no!" and laughter, as Sonny adds, "You missed!" A score overlay appears, 'SONNY 10 - 0 SHIZUKA', highlighting a playful competition. Sonny continues, "But above all else, this one's literally right here, it's a gift from God. You must commit." The group laughs, and Sonny playfully sprays water from his bottle onto the plants. The woman exclaims, "Yes! Amazing!" when Sonny asks, "Do you think I'm brave?" to which she replies, "A little bit!" Sonny reflects on food, stating, "I've tried delicious food from all over the world... Food that other people cook." The woman asks, "Why are they gone?" as Sonny concludes, "But there's nothing like the sense of satisfaction that comes from getting out there... and catching your own meal." The video ends with a close-up of a large green leaf with red flowers and a brief shot of a yellow grasshopper held in a black mesh net, as the woman says, "I'll try another one. I'm trying," and Sonny confirms, "There's a grasshopper."

The video opens with a close-up of a large green leaf, where a bright yellow grasshopper is perched. A male voice (Man in Blue Shirt with Headband) narrates, describing the grasshopper as looking "a little sad" and "depressed," wanting to be caught and join its friends, urging action to "swoop it up, like the hand of God." The scene transitions to a wide shot of a golden rice field under an overcast sky, where the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband crouches beside the Woman in Yellow Dress, both examining crops. The woman exclaims, "Yes! It's in my hand!" followed by a playful "It flew right out of my hand!" The man responds, "You did it! There it is, look, you got a huge one!" and asks, "Don't you feel that adrenaline rush? Don't you feel amazing like you accomplished something?" The woman proudly declares, "I am a hunter." They share a high-five as a score overlay appears: 'SONNY 6 - 1 SHIZUKA', accompanied by upbeat, driving background music. The camera cuts to a close-up of a white container with green plant matter. The scene shifts to a panoramic aerial view of the golden rice fields, a small town, and distant mountains. The video then transitions to a traditional Japanese house. The male voice (Man in Blue Shirt with Headband), identified as Masamichi, narrates, "We've got today's secret ingredient. Masamichi brings us to a local home," introducing "Japan's riskiest food-making procedure." Two men are seen pounding rice in a large wooden mortar with mallets, joined by the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband and the Woman in Yellow Dress, who also participate, accompanied by the rhythmic sounds of rice pounding. The scene ends with a close-up of the wooden mortar, showing the rice being pounded into a paste.

The video opens with the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband, wearing a white 7-Eleven headband and a dark blue polka-dotted shirt, holding a large wooden mallet and speaking animatedly, explaining that "there's two people with hammers and then there's one unlucky person who has to stick their hand in there." He points to the Man in Blue Shirt and White Headband, asking "How you doing?", to which the Woman in Yellow Dress, also holding a mallet, playfully interjects "Be scared." The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband agrees, "You should be," followed by laughter. He then narrates, introducing "mochi, a sticky, gooey classic," and detailing its preparation from soaking rice overnight, straining, and steaming, before it's pounded in a large wooden mortar. The Man in Blue Shirt and White Headband, crouching beside the mortar, expresses his goal: "Man, if you trust me, I want to see how tight of a pattern we can get," as rhythmic pounding sounds begin. The Woman in Yellow Dress humorously declines participation in Japanese, "Watashi ga yarimasen" and "Yaranai," before a countdown of "3, 2, 1, pound!" initiates intense, rapid pounding. The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband takes turns, swinging the mallet with effort and enthusiasm, grunting with exertion, while close-up shots reveal the sticky, white rice being mashed. The group laughs and interacts playfully, with the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband exclaiming "Hey!" after a particularly strong pound. He then describes the "crazy sticky" product, noting "it won't even come off his hands." The Woman in Yellow Dress, smiling, adds "Oh my goodness, this is more than I can chew," as the rice is shaped into a long, smooth mochi strip. The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband invites them to "Let's try it out," and all three participants taste the mochi, with sounds of eating and an appreciative "Oh" heard, all set against the backdrop of a traditional Japanese house with sliding doors and Japanese signage.

The video opens with a group of three—the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband, the Woman in Yellow Dress, and a man in a navy blue t-shirt—standing outside a traditional Japanese building, tasting small pieces of white mochi with expressions of delight, accompanied by laughter and sounds of eating. The Man in Blue Shirt describes the mochi as "so weird, it's like the unrefined version, it's so sticky and clumpy," while the Woman in Yellow Dress adds, "Pure mochi, freshly pounded rice cake, with no flavoring added," before playfully responding "No" to a question about eating something on her arm. The scene transitions to a close-up of hands skillfully stretching and folding the elastic mochi, then shifts to a cozy Traditional Japanese Kitchen where the Woman in Green Cardigan prepares a meal, stretching mochi over a bowl and ladling steaming broth. Over a backdrop of traditional Japanese music, a narrator explains that "Mochi is a carby, amorphous base," detailing how it can be made into "mochi soup" with vegetable broth, topped with "red bean paste," coated in "soybean flour" for a "yellow vibrant dessert," or topped with "natto," which is described as "Japan's infamous sticky fermented soybean." The camera pans over a beautifully arranged table laden with these various dishes, including golden-brown fried mochi and natto over rice. The group, now seated around a Low Wooden Table on Tatami Mats in a Traditional Japanese Dining Room, laughs and enjoys the communal meal. The Man in Blue Shirt asks a new diner, "what's your name?", who replies in a Japanese accent, "My name is Yujiro Iwata," and clarifies, "I can't speak English," despite the Man in Blue Shirt's initial assumption. The narrator introduces him as "Mr. Yuchiro" and his wife, Asami, joining the meal. The video then shows a close-up of a Grasshopper Jar filled with live yellow grasshoppers, as the Man in Blue Shirt asks the Woman in Yellow Dress, "Have you ever had a bug?", to which she responds "Never" with an "Oh no." The narrator concludes, "This is what we came here for. The grasshopper is boiled in...", before returning to the group's joyful dining experience.

Sautéed Grasshoppers.description: Glistening, dark-glazed grasshoppers, cooked in a pan and served in small blue-and-white patterned bowls. A narrator describes their preparation involving hot water, stir-frying with sugar, soy sauce, and sake to create a teriyaki sauce. They are later described by a diner as 'really good,' 'sweet and salty,' 'like teriyaki grasshoppers,' and having a 'crunchy, papery' texture with 'no gooiness.'

Man in Blue Shirt with Headband.description: A man in a blue polka-dotted shirt and a white headband with a 7-Eleven logo, seated at a low wooden table, reacting with surprise and amusement as he eats mochi topped with natto. He has a distinct voice, asking questions about the dish, describing its "fermented," "stringy," and "slimy" texture, and later, when trying fried mochi, noting its "sticky texture," "sweet," and "nutty, earthy" flavor.

Traditional Japanese Dining Room.description: A dimly lit room with tatami flooring and wooden walls, where the group enjoys a communal meal, enhanced by the distinct sound of a gong/chime at key transitions.

Woman in Yellow Dress.description: A woman in a yellow long-sleeve top, seated at the dining table, laughing heartily and covering her mouth. She has a distinct voice, confirming details about the natto, describing the fried mochi as a "chewy mouthful," expressing delight with a long "Mmmmmm" and stating, "That's some good stuff," and providing cultural context about eating natto.

Tatami Mats.description: Traditional Japanese floor mats made of woven straw, upon which the diners sit on cushions around the low wooden table, adding to the authentic atmosphere.

Man in Blue T-Shirt.description: A man in a blue t-shirt, seated beside the Woman in Yellow Dress, watching the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband with a smile, participating in the meal and conversation.

Low Wooden Table.description: A low, polished wooden table set on tatami mats, surrounded by diners seated on cushions, with an array of small blue-and-white patterned bowls and plates filled with mochi, natto, and broth.

Blue-and-White Patterned Bowls.description: Small ceramic bowls with blue floral patterns, used for serving individual portions of broth, natto, and other side dishes at the dining table.

Woman in Green Cardigan.description: A woman in a green cardigan over a black top, seated at the table, smiling and engaging in the communal meal. She has a distinct voice, offering advice on mixing the natto: "The more you mix it, the healthier it gets."

Fried Mochi.description: Golden-brown, crispy mochi pieces coated in breadcrumbs, topped with a dark, glossy sauce, described in the audio as a "chewy mouthful," "sticky," "sweet," and "nutty, earthy."

Natto.description: A traditional Japanese dish of fermented soybeans, shown with a glossy, stringy texture, described in the audio as "fermented," "stringy," and "slimy," with its smell being "more intense than the flavor."

Mochi.description: A soft, white, chewy rice cake, shown being stretched and folded, served in various forms—plain, topped with natto, or fried—emphasized by audio descriptions of its "stringy," "slimy," and "sticky" texture.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a chef stirring a pot on a gas stove, then transitions to a dramatic close-up of a pan filled with glistening, sautéed grasshoppers, their legs and bodies coated in a dark, savory glaze. A narrator explains their preparation: first, they are cleaned with hot water to remove any dirt, then stir-fried in a blend of sugar, soy sauce, and sake, creating a homemade teriyaki sauce. The scene shifts to a traditional Japanese dining room with tatami mats and wooden decor, where a group of diners, including the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband, the Woman in Yellow Dress, and a Man in Blue T-Shirt, are seated around a low wooden table. The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband, wearing a white headband with a 7-Eleven logo, is handed a small blue-and-white patterned bowl of the cooked grasshoppers. A man's voice (likely his) encourages, "I want you to grab a few. Should I help you? I can dish you up some." A woman's voice (likely the Woman in Yellow Dress) hesitantly says, "I just like this," while another man humorously advises, "Don't look at them. That's the last thing you should do is look at them." The Woman in Green Cardigan exclaims "Delicious, delicious!" in Japanese. The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband reacts with wide-eyed curiosity and amusement, using chopsticks to pick up a grasshopper, examining it closely. The man's voice continues to encourage, "Alright, we'll do it together. Listen, look at this. I got two right here. Let's do this." The Woman in Yellow Dress laughs heartily, covering her mouth, and asks, "Can I eat it if I don't look at it?" to which the man playfully responds, "Yeah, I could drop it in your mouth." She exclaims, "Oh god," followed by laughter from the group. The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband then declares, "Alright, I'm gonna try it out," and after a moment, with traditional Japanese music playing in the background, he takes a bite. His expression shifts from surprise to delight as he exclaims, "Oh, that's really good! It's sweet and salty. It's like teriyaki grasshoppers!" The Woman in Green Cardigan again says "Delicious!" and the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband elaborates, "It's just crunchy, papery, there's no gooiness. It's a pretty good sell." The Woman in Yellow Dress agrees, "It's not bad, it's not bad. Mhm, mhm," as the convivial atmosphere continues, with the Man in Blue T-Shirt watching with a smile. The table is set with various small blue-and-white patterned bowls, plates of mochi, natto, and broth, emphasizing the traditional Japanese dining setup, all under soft, natural lighting.

The video opens with a close-up of a black ceramic bowl containing soft, white mochi topped with glistening, stringy natto. A distinct gong sound punctuates the scene as a Man in Blue Shirt with Headband asks, "Right here, this is mochi with natto?" The Woman in Yellow Dress confirms, "Natto. Natto." The scene transitions to a traditional Japanese dining room with tatami mats and wooden walls, where the group is seated around a low wooden table. The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband, wearing a 7-Eleven headband, observes, "It doesn't smell bad, but to me it's obviously fermented," as he picks up a piece of mochi with chopsticks, stretching the natto strings dramatically, reacting with wide-eyed surprise. He exclaims, "Oh, yes. It's stringy. It's so unique," and then notes, "Oh, he just mixes it up like that." The Woman in Yellow Dress adds, "Mix it up," while the Woman in Green Cardigan chimes in, "The more you mix it, the healthier it gets." The Woman in Yellow Dress laughs heartily, covering her mouth, amused by his reaction. Close-ups emphasize the gooey texture of the natto, which the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband describes as "definitely slimy. It's like slime on slime crime over here. It's all just a texture sensation." The Woman in Yellow Dress, however, states, "I'm actually really liking this," adding that while "foreign people think it's such a strange food, but in Japan they'll eat it like every morning." Another gong sound signals a shift as the group focuses on a plate of golden-brown fried mochi, coated in breadcrumbs and topped with a dark, glossy sauce. The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband declares, "Right here, it's our dessert. Let's try it out." The Woman in Yellow Dress picks up a piece, examining it closely before taking a bite, exclaiming, "Whoa, that's a chewy mouthful," and then, with a long, satisfied "Mmmmmm," declares, "That's some good stuff." The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband follows suit, chewing thoughtfully, describing it as having the "same sticky texture," "a little bit sweet, and then a little bit... nutty, earthy?" The Woman in Yellow Dress confirms, "Nutty." The communal, playful atmosphere of the meal is evident through their animated conversation and enjoyment of the unique textures and flavors, all set against the authentic backdrop of the traditional Japanese dining room with its calligraphy and decorative mask.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a description of fried mochi as a traditional Japanese dessert with subtle sweetness, served on a ceramic plate. The scene shifts to a traditional Japanese dining room, adorned with tatami mats, shoji screens, a large colorful mask, and framed calligraphy, where a Man in Blue Shirt with Headband, a Woman in Yellow Dress, and a Man in Blue T-Shirt converse. The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband recounts his return to Fukushima after working in Tokyo to support his hometown following an earthquake eight years prior, a sentiment echoed by others who returned to help rebuild. The video then showcases a display of traditional Japanese dolls and artifacts, followed by the group outside a traditional wooden gate. An aerial view reveals rural Japanese landscapes with golden rice fields and distant mountains, leading to a narrow street in a traditional town with wooden buildings and a river. Before departing Fukushima, a speaker highlights an opportunity to try a rare freshwater fish native to the region, while koi fish swim in a pond. Later, the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband is seen in a kitchen, gesturing towards a large pot, and the segment concludes with a close-up of a traditional Japanese lantern with black calligraphy. The scene transitions to a 146-year-old restaurant, hotel, and hot spring specializing in traditional Japanese foods, where a man in a blue polka-dotted shirt and a woman in a yellow long-sleeve top descend into a kitchen. The man inquires about koi fish, learning from the chef and the woman's translation that 'Koi' refers to carp fish sourced from local rivers. Colorful bowls of raw fish topped with egg yolk are shown, alongside vibrant koi fish in a pond, symbolizing persistence, determination, wealth, success, and good fortune. The narrative takes a dramatic turn as a metal hatch opens to reveal a dark pit, from which the chef scoops using a strainer, demonstrating a traditional cooking method. The man expresses surprise, having initially expected to eat ornamental koi, but proceeds to try the dish. The Chef in White Uniform clarifies that while ornamental koi are not for consumption, a separate breed is raised for eating, prompting the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband to playfully request the 'cutest one.' A murky underwater view of a dark fish precedes the chef rinsing a dark, scaled fish. A narrator explains that these fish will be prepared as 'Koi Umani,' a dish of large fish pieces simmered for nine hours with soy sauce, sugar, and homemade sake. The chef stirs a large pot of richly braised meat, revealing its tender, glossy texture. The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband then walks through a traditional Japanese interior with wooden beams and paper screens, accompanied by serene traditional Japanese music.

Man in Blue Shirt with Headband.description: A man in a blue polka-dotted shirt and a white headband with a 7-Eleven logo, initially conversing with the chef, then walking through a traditional Japanese interior with wooden beams and paper screens, appearing curious and engaged, and heard asking if the edible fish are 'still cute' and playfully requesting the 'cutest one' for preparation.

Woman in Yellow Dress.description: A woman in a yellow long-sleeve top, descending a staircase with a smile, engaging in conversation with the chef and the man, her expression animated and curious, and explaining that koi is a carp fish sourced from local rivers.

Traditional Japanese Dining Room.description: A dimly lit room with tatami flooring, wooden walls, and sliding shoji screens, decorated with framed calligraphy and a large, colorful mask, where the group enjoys a communal meal and engages in conversation about the mochi and the impact of an 8-year-old event, specifically the Fukushima earthquake and the return of residents to rebuild.

Tatami Mats.description: Traditional Japanese floor mats made of woven straw, upon which the diners sit on cushions around the low wooden table, adding to the authentic atmosphere.

Traditional Gate.description: A wooden gate with a tiled roof and Japanese characters, possibly marking the entrance to a ryokan or temple, with a stone lantern in front, seen as the group exits, further contextualizing the Fukushima setting.

Ceramic Doll Display.description: A collection of traditional Japanese dolls, including a tall wooden figure and a seated ceramic doll, displayed on a wooden shelf, emphasizing cultural heritage.

Koi Fish.description: Colorful koi fish with orange, white, and black patterns swimming in a pond, initially presented as the ornamental variety, but the audio clarifies that a separate breed of koi is specifically raised for eating, which will be prepared into a dish called Koi Umani.

Koi Pond.description: A clear pond filled with colorful koi fish, located beside a traditional Japanese building, reflecting the serene and cultural setting.

Framed Calligraphy.description: Handwritten Japanese calligraphy on white paper, mounted on the wooden wall, including characters like '向流', indicating the name or theme of the establishment.

Fried Mochi.description: Golden-brown, crispy mochi pieces coated in breadcrumbs, topped with a dark, glossy sauce, served on a textured gray plate, described by a speaker as a 'traditional Japanese dessert' with 'subtle sweetness' as diners taste and enjoy it.

Japanese Signage.description: Wooden signs with black Japanese characters, including '向流' (Mukiryu), mounted on a traditional building, indicating the name of the establishment.

Traditional Japanese House.description: A traditional Japanese house with wooden beams, sliding doors, and a small blue sign with Japanese text, serving as the backdrop for the outdoor mochi-making activity.

Large Colorful Mask.description: A large, traditional Japanese mask with exaggerated features, including wide eyes and a red mouth, displayed in the dining room as a decorative element.

Narrow Street in Traditional Town.description: A narrow street lined with traditional wooden buildings, a river running alongside, and a bridge connecting both sides, surrounded by lush green hills, visually representing the traditional setting in Fukushima mentioned in the audio.

Man in Blue T-Shirt.description: A man in a blue t-shirt, seated beside the Woman in Yellow Dress, smiling and participating in the meal and conversation, including the discussion about the Fukushima earthquake and rebuilding.

Low Wooden Table.description: A low, polished wooden table set on tatami mats, surrounded by diners seated on cushions, with an array of small blue-and-white patterned bowls and plates filled with mochi, natto, and broth.

Red Lantern.description: A bright red, traditional Japanese lantern with a rounded top, placed on a wooden railing near a river, adding to the cultural ambiance.

Blue-and-White Patterned Bowls.description: Small ceramic bowls with blue floral patterns, used for serving individual portions of broth, natto, and other side dishes at the dining table.

Aerial View of Rural Japan.description: An expansive aerial shot of a rural Japanese landscape with golden rice fields, small towns, and distant mountains under an overcast sky, providing context for the discussion about Fukushima and its rebuilding.

Chef in White Uniform.description: A chef wearing a white uniform, hairnet, and glasses, actively engaged in cooking, using a strainer to scoop from a deep pit, rinsing a fish, and stirring a pot of braised meat, demonstrating traditional techniques in a professional kitchen.

Metal Hatch.description: A close-up of a metal hatch with a diamond plate pattern being opened, revealing a dark, industrial-looking space beneath, suggesting a hidden or underground cooking element.

Deep Pit.description: A dark, deep pit in the kitchen from which the chef uses a strainer to scoop ingredients, with steam rising, suggesting a traditional or unique cooking technique.

Dark Fish.description: A murky, dark-colored fish, possibly a catfish, seen swimming slowly in greenish water, later rinsed by the chef, and identified in the audio as the type of fish that will be prepared into the dish 'Koi Umani'.

Simmering Braised Meat.description: A large pot of rich, glossy braised meat simmering in a dark broth, being stirred with a ladle and lifted to show its tender, well-cooked texture, identified by the audio as 'Koi Umani,' a dish made from big pieces of fish simmered for nine hours with soy sauce, sugar, and homemade sake until tender with a thick broth.

Traditional Japanese Interior.description: An interior with wooden beams, paper screens, and warm lighting, through which the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband walks, creating a serene and culturally authentic atmosphere.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of golden-brown fried mochi, described by a speaker as a "traditional Japanese dessert" with "subtle sweetness," served on a rustic gray ceramic plate. The scene transitions to a traditional Japanese dining room, cozy with tatami mats, shoji screens, and traditional decor including a large colorful mask and framed calligraphy, where the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband (wearing a 7-Eleven headband and blue polka-dotted shirt), the Woman in Yellow Dress, and the Man in Blue T-Shirt engage in lively conversation while eating. Amidst background music, they discuss an event from 8 years ago, with the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband speaking Japanese, translated as having worked in Tokyo but returning to Fukushima after the earthquake to support his hometown. A narrator confirms that "like Yuichiro and his wife, many others returned to Fukushima, helping to rebuild it." The camera then pans to a display of traditional Japanese dolls and artifacts, emphasizing cultural heritage, before showing the group outside a traditional wooden gate with Japanese characters. A wide aerial view of rural Japanese landscapes with golden rice fields and distant mountains, followed by a narrow street in a traditional town with wooden buildings and a river, sets the scene. Before leaving Fukushima, a speaker highlights a "unique opportunity to try out a rare freshwater fish, native to this part of Japan," as koi fish swim in a pond. The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband is later seen in a kitchen, gesturing toward a large pot, and the video concludes with a close-up of a traditional Japanese lantern with black calligraphy, reinforcing the cultural and culinary journey, interspersed with Japanese greetings like "Hajimemashite," "Yoroshiku onegaishimasu," and "Thank you."

The segment opens with a view into a warmly lit souvenir shop, showcasing traditional Japanese goods and a herringbone-patterned floor, accompanied by traditional Japanese background music. A narrator introduces the setting as a 146-year-old restaurant, hotel, and hot spring specializing in traditional Japanese foods. A man in a blue polka-dotted shirt and a woman in a yellow long-sleeve top descend a narrow staircase into a kitchen, where the man animatedly speaks to a chef in a white uniform. The man asks about a dish on the menu and then inquires, "What is Koi in Japanese?", to which the chef responds in Japanese. The woman translates, explaining that "Koi is a carp fish" about "this big" and sourced from "local rivers in Aizu." The camera then shows colorful, patterned bowls filled with raw fish topped with a bright orange egg yolk, followed by vibrant koi fish swimming in a pond, with on-screen text highlighting their symbolism: 'VIBRANT', 'DETERMINATION', 'GOOD FORTUNE', and 'SUCCESS'. The narrator elaborates that koi symbolize persistence, determination, wealth, success, and good fortune, noting he's seen them in picturesque ponds but "never on a dinner table." The music shifts to a dramatic, suspenseful tone as a hand opens a metal hatch, revealing a dark pit below. The chef uses a strainer to scoop from it, demonstrating a traditional cooking method. The man points toward the pit with surprise and amusement, humorously confessing, "Can I tell you something funny? I came here because I thought we're gonna eat one of those like koi goldfish... I'm doing it anyways!" He then clarifies, "but I don't think it's that kind of fish. What kind of koi fish do you have?" The segment concludes with the man and woman laughing together, clearly enjoying the experience, as the chef continues to demonstrate the unique cooking technique in the industrial kitchen setting, with the background music returning to a lighter, conversational tone.

The video opens in a professional kitchen with a cheerful interaction between the Chef in White Uniform, who gestures animatedly, and the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband. A female voice clarifies that while the vibrant, colorful koi fish seen swimming in a pond are ornamental, a separate breed is specifically raised for eating. The Man in Blue Shirt with Headband, appearing curious, asks if these edible fish are "still cute," prompting a "Kawaii" response, and then playfully requests the "cutest one." The scene shifts to a murky underwater view of a dark fish, possibly a catfish, moving slowly, before returning to the kitchen where the Chef uses a long-handled strainer in a deep, dark pit, suggesting a traditional cooking method. He then rinses a dark, scaled fish under running water. A male narrator explains that "these fish" will become "Koi Umani," a dish of big fish pieces simmered for nine hours with soy sauce, sugar, and homemade sake. The camera highlights a large pot of richly braised meat, which the chef stirs and lifts to reveal its tender, glossy texture, embodying the described Koi Umani. After a close-up of a glass of water, the Man in Blue Shirt with Headband walks through a traditional Japanese interior with wooden beams and paper screens, his expression engaged, as serene traditional Japanese music begins, transitioning the atmosphere from culinary craftsmanship to cultural context.

event_blocks.summary: In a traditional Japanese-style room with a framed calligraphy piece and a bronze bull statue, a woman in a yellow dress and a man in a blue shirt with a headband sit at a glossy black table. The man, entering and declaring "Everyone quiet, I'm in character," greets viewers for a food show, introducing a "koi fish" dish with a "Tada!" sound effect. The table is set with red lidded bowls, shot glasses, and a green sake bottle. The man describes their private room setting, noting a "7-inch TV" and the "beautiful background," while the woman mentions a "T-station." The woman laughs while holding a patterned object. The man reveals a dark, glossy braised meat dish with a "caramelized glaze on the outside" from the bowls. Both use chopsticks to eat and sip from shot glasses. The woman explains the room's dual purpose for sleeping, stating, "This is a room you can sleep in. So you eat here and then you put your futons out and then you sleep," with the man adding, "Sleep or pass out," and the woman agreeing, "Probably both." After a close-up of the saucy meat, they continue eating. The man exclaims, "Oh, that's nice. Wow, that's smooth," after tasting, and speculates, "Do you think that's egg? Maybe it's grasshopper," which the woman rejects with, "No, it better not be! Anything but grasshopper!" The man concludes, "Alright, let's try it out. Cheers!" as they continue their meal amidst upbeat background music.

Man in Blue Shirt with Headband.description: A man in a blue polka-dotted shirt and a white headband with a 7-Eleven logo, entering the scene, gesturing while speaking, then sitting to eat from a red bowl with chopsticks and sipping from a shot glass. He speaks extensively, introducing himself and the "food show," describing the food as "koi fish" with a "Tada!" sound effect, detailing the "restaurant's suite" and its features ("beautiful background," "7-inch TV"), adding to the room's description ("Sleep or pass out"), expressing enjoyment ("Oh, that's nice. Wow, that's smooth"), describing the food's texture ("caramelized glaze on the outside"), speculating on ingredients ("Do you think that's egg?", "Maybe it's grasshopper"), and concluding with "Alright, let's try it out. Cheers!".

Woman in Yellow Dress.description: A woman in a yellow long-sleeve top, seated at a glossy black table, smiling and laughing as she interacts with the man, holding a patterned object, and later eating from a red bowl with chopsticks and sipping from a shot glass. She speaks, explaining the room's function ("This is a room you can sleep in..."), agreeing with the man ("Probably both"), and expressing uncertainty and strong dislike regarding food ingredients ("I have no idea," "No, it better not be! Anything but grasshopper!").

Traditional Japanese Interior.description: A room with shoji screens, wooden beams, and warm lighting, featuring a glossy black table, a framed calligraphy piece, and a bronze bull statue, creating an authentic and cozy atmosphere. The man describes it as "this restaurant's suite, a private room," with a "beautiful background" and a "7-inch TV," while the woman mentions a "T-station" and explains it's "a room you can sleep in."

Framed Calligraphy.description: A framed piece of Japanese calligraphy on the wall, featuring bold black characters, contributing to the traditional aesthetic of the space.

A man in a blue polka-dotted shirt and a 7-Eleven headband enters, declaring, "Everyone quiet, I'm in character," before greeting viewers with "Hey guys, welcome back to the food show." He gestures animatedly as he speaks, introducing what he claims is a "koi fish" (referring to the food) with a "Tada!" sound effect. The man describes their setting as "this restaurant's suite, a private room," noting the "beautiful background" and a "7-inch TV," while the woman adds there's a "T-station." So you eat here and then you put your futons out and then you sleep," to which the man adds, "Sleep or pass out." She responds, "Probably both." A close-up shows the rich, saucy meat. event_blocks.summary: Within a traditional Japanese interior, a Man in Blue Shirt with Headband, identifiable by his 7-Eleven headband, and a Woman in Yellow Dress are seated at a glossy black table, partaking in a braised carp dish served in red ceramic bowls. The man describes the carp as "crumbly on the inside" yet coated in a "thick soy-based glaze" that is "a little sweet, a little salty," while the woman likens its taste to a cooked egg yolk and corrects his assertion about carp scales, leading to his playful remark, "I'm quitting my show." They both commend the dish as "very nice" and "oishii," appreciating its tenderness from boiling and its "powerful flavor" devoid of any "strange aftertaste," while sipping from shot glasses, presumably filled with sake from a green sake bottle. Close-ups highlight the carp's glossy, tender texture. The setting transitions to an aerial perspective of a golden rice field in Kitakata, Fukushima, framed by distant mountains and traditional houses, then focuses on moss-covered roof tiles and a traditional gate adorned with a stone lantern, accompanied by melancholic music featuring female vocals. A male narrator then introduces Fukushima as a region repeatedly struck by disaster, now a symbol of Japan.

Man in Blue Shirt with Headband.description: A man in a blue polka-dotted shirt and a white headband with a 7-Eleven logo, seated at a glossy black table, eating braised meat from a red bowl with chopsticks, gesturing while speaking in a clear voice, describing the food as 'crumbly,' 'soy-based glaze,' 'sweet,' 'salty,' 'comes off real nice,' 'so soft,' 'tenderized,' and having a 'powerful flavor.' He also humorously states 'I don't think carp have scales' and 'I'm quitting my show,' and says 'oishii' (delicious).

She speaks in a clear voice, describing the food as tasting 'like an actual chicken egg, like the yolk that's been cooked,' 'very nice,' 'never expected the carp was going to be this good,' 'doesn't have any strange aftertaste,' 'very tender,' and 'delicious.' She also corrects the man, stating 'They do!' when he questions if carp have scales.
